") [Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223/notes) Abstract -------- Lignocellulose, as Earth’s most abundant renewable biomass, represents a crucial resource for the production of biofuels and biochemicals, it is of great significance for sustainable development. Microbial degradation offers a promising pathway for transforming lignocellulose into valuable products. This review explores the diversity and classification of lignocellulose-degrading microorganisms, focusing on fungi and bacteria and their respective enzyme systems responsible for breaking down cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Key factors influencing degradation efficiency, including environmental conditions, substrate complexity, and microbial interactions, are thoroughly analyzed. Limitations in microbial degradation are also discussed, notably the need for identifying high-activity strains. Additionally, the review outlines future research directions, emphasizing the application of advanced technologies such as genomics, synthetic biology, and machine learning to optimize microbial degradation processes. These insights aim to enhance lignocellulose utilization efficiency, fostering its broader industrial and agricultural applications. Keywords: [lignocellulose degradation](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=lignocellulose+degradation); [microbial enzyme systems](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=microbial+enzyme+systems); [biomass utilization](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=biomass+utilization); [sustainable biofuels](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=sustainable+biofuels) 1. Introduction --------------- In recent years, the global focus on reducing greenhouse gas emissions and combating climate change has intensified, leading to a stronger emphasis on sustainable development. Lignocellulose, one of the most abundant renewable biomass resources on Earth [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B1-sustainability-17-04223)]. Many solid wastes from crops such as rice husks, straw and rice stalks are rich in lignocellulose. The burning and discarding of these solid wastes lead to the waste of renewable resources and hinder the sustainable utilization of resources. Therefore, the effective utilization of lignocellulose has attracted widespread attention. Lignocellulose, composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, is the primary component of agricultural waste and a significant non-food biomass resource, providing an abundant source of C5 and C6 sugars without affecting the food supply [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B2-sustainability-17-04223),[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B3-sustainability-17-04223)]. Therefore, utilizing lignocellulose as a feedstock to convert it into valuable biomass products, such as biofuels (an alternative to traditional fossil fuels) and other renewable materials, has emerged as a crucial green and sustainable strategy to mitigate global warming while repurposing agricultural and industrial waste [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B4-sustainability-17-04223),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B5-sustainability-17-04223)]. The efficient utilization of lignocellulose largely depends on biorefining processes to synthesize biofuels, biomaterials, and other value-added bioproducts. In biorefineries, a critical step in processing biomass is the pretreatment of its basic components ([Figure 1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#fig_body_display_sustainability-17-04223-f001)) [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B6-sustainability-17-04223),[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B7-sustainability-17-04223)]. Compared to energy-intensive physical methods, chemical pretreatment with acids, alkalis, and organic solvents is more efficient and adaptable to various types of biomass, making it one of the most promising approaches. However, this method will inevitably generate intermediate toxic by-products. For instance, the initial products of acid pretreatment of hemicellulose, such as hexose and pentose, will further decompose to form furfural and hydroxymethylfurfural, which will inhibit subsequent enzymatic hydrolysis and microbial fermentation. The unfavorable by-products obtained in the alkaline pretreatment process are relatively fewer, but the acid and alkaline pretreatment liquids are highly corrosive, imposing higher requirements on the reaction equipment and causing certain pollution to the environment. Moreover, organic solvents with lower toxicity usually have flammability, volatility, and explosiveness, which not only have a high risk factor but also have a high recovery cost. For the sake of environmental safety, economic efficiency, and the fact that no investment in high-energy equipment is required, low-cost biological pretreatment with fewer chemical additives is gradually replacing chemical methods as a more attractive option [[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B8-sustainability-17-04223)]. Biological pretreatment primarily utilizes microorganisms and enzymes to degrade biomass in a gentler, more environmentally friendly manner. Compared to enzyme pretreatment, microorganisms offer greater functional diversity and resilience to environmental factors such as temperature and pH, resulting in higher pretreatment efficiency during anaerobic digestion [[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B9-sustainability-17-04223)]. ![Image 13: Sustainability 17 04223 g001](https://www.mdpi.com/sustainability/sustainability-17-04223/article_deploy/html/images/sustainability-17-04223-g001-550.jpg)[](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#fig_body_display_sustainability-17-04223-f001) **Figure 1.** Pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B6-sustainability-17-04223)]. However, lignocellulosic-based microbial treatment methods also face the challenge of high-cost lignocellulases, as shown in [Table 1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#table_body_display_sustainability-17-04223-t001), for the relevant characteristics of enzyme production processes, which are critical for evaluating the performance of any biotechnological process, and comparing the composition of enzyme production costs derived from these process designs, where raw material and capital-related costs are often the main drivers of enzyme production costs. The data provided in [Table 1](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#table_body_display_sustainability-17-04223-t001) indicate that the low-cost cellulase from Trichoderma reesei is obtained through a combination of extremely high enzyme yields, high enzyme titers, and high volumetric productivities. It can be seen that improving process parameters and optimizing the cellulase production process is one of the ways to increase the degradation rate of lignocellulose. However, the most fundamental approach is to screen out high-yield and stable enzyme-producing microorganisms (such as T. reesei, which is a typical lignocellulose-degrading microorganism that secretes a high-level cellulase mixture) to increase the enzyme yield per unit fermentation volume and reduce production costs [[10](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B10-sustainability-17-04223)]. **Table 1.** Process characteristics and enzyme cost composition of selected techno-economic analyses of industrial enzyme production. ![Image 14](https://pub.mdpi-res.com/img/table.png)[](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#table_body_display_sustainability-17-04223-t001) In nature, lignocellulose degradation is achieved through the synergistic actions of fungi and bacteria, which secrete cellulases, hemicellulases, and ligninases to break down cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, either simultaneously or selectively [[17](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B17-sustainability-17-04223)]. In this context, the study of microbial degradation of lignocellulose has become a hot topic in the fields of ecology, resource utilization, and sustainable development. Since the earliest fungal degradation of cellulose, efforts have been made to unravel the microbial degradation mechanism of lignocellulose [[18](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B18-sustainability-17-04223)]. However, lignocellulose has an exceptionally complex structure, and its degradation demands the coordinated activity of various microorganisms and a diverse array of degradative enzymes. To date, many microorganisms involved in this process remain poorly classified, and their metabolic pathways are not fully understood. Furthermore, the cooperative degradation mechanisms among different microorganisms require further investigation [[19](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B19-sustainability-17-04223)]. This paper reviews recent advances in the study of lignocellulose-degrading microorganisms and enzyme systems, along with an analysis of the various factors influencing lignocellulose degradation. These insights aim to establish a theoretical foundation for the efficient utilization of lignocellulose. 2. Diversity and Classification of Lignocellulose-Degrading Microorganisms -------------------------------------------------------------------------- In nature, lignocellulose degradation is a complex and orderly process, involving diverse microorganisms that collaborate to produce enzymes which attack, depolymerize, and degrade lignocellulosic polymers. These microorganisms are both highly diverse and abundant, with the capacity to proliferate rapidly under favorable conditions [[20](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B20-sustainability-17-04223)]. #### 2.1. Classification of Fungi in Lignocellulose Degradation Fungi are the most extensively reported and deeply studied microorganisms in lignocellulose degradation and depolymerization. Research has shown that lignocellulose-degrading fungi can be classified into three major categories—white-rot, brown-rot, and soft-rot fungi—based on the type and extent of degradation they induce [[21](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B21-sustainability-17-04223),[22](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/17/9/4223#B22-sustainability-17-04223)]. [![Image 8: Elsevier](https://sdfestaticassets-us-east-1.sciencedirectassets.com/prod/bbcebc374bd034f2b69007c2d8fa1a7860b68c43/image/elsevier-non-solus.svg)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/next-sustainability "Go to Next Sustainability on ScienceDirect") [Next Sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/next-sustainability "Go to Next Sustainability on ScienceDirect") ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- [Volume 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/next-sustainability/vol/5/suppl/C "Go to table of contents for this volume/issue"), 2025, 100133 [![Image 9: Next Sustainability](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823624X00033-cov150h.gif)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/next-sustainability/vol/5/suppl/C) Biological pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass as a sustainable option for biofuel production ================================================================================================= Author links open overlay panel Naveen Kumar a b, Himanshu Saini a, Neeraj K.Aggarwal b, Nishu Jangra b, Kavita Dhiman b, Ishu Sangwan b Show more Outline Add to Mendeley Share Cite [https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nxsust.2025.100133](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nxsust.2025.100133 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2949823625000364&orderBeanReset=true) Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Open access Abstract -------- Biofuels derived from lignocellulosic material are renewable, sustainable, and ecologically friendly, presenting a significant alternative to fossil fuels. However, before saccharification, pretreatment is an important stage in arranging the lignocellulose layers. The biological treatment of lignocellulose, utilizing microbes such as bacteria and fungi, is increasingly prevalent due to its financial and environmental benefits. Choosing the right microbial consortia with care is essential to effectively pretreating biomass. Our collection of exceptionally promising bacteria and/or fungi is capable of manufacturing a range of extracellular enzymes, such as lignases, cellulases, and hemicellulases. It can effectively treat lignocellulosic biomass biologically in order to produce biofuels. This review article offers an in-depth exploration of biological pretreatment strategies for lignocellulosic biomass, highlighting key mechanisms, innovative technologies, influencing factors, and the latest advancements shaping current research in the field. * [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000224) * [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823624000394) Keywords -------- Lignocellulosic biomass Biological pretreatment Bacteria Fungi Biofuel production 1. Introduction --------------- Bioethanol production is recognized as an acceptable substitute for fossil fuels. The fermentation process turns sugar into ethanol. Remarkable fermentation technology is regarded as one of the key components of a successful industrial upscaling of bioethanol production. Furthermore, the availability of resources, economic viability, and environmental friendliness are increasingly taken seriously as the main determinants of the success of bioethanol production [[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib1). Lignocellulosic biomass (LCB) is widely available in nature, making it a sustainable and eco-friendly alternative source of energy. Its composition varies depending on the source of the biomass and includes 40–50 % cellulose, 25–30 % hemicellulose, 15–20 % lignin, pectin, nitrogen, and a few other compounds that are inorganic [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib2). Cellulose gives lignocelluloses their fibrous and crystallized structure. It also creates a complex base on which other materials can deposit and interact. Cellulose serves as a structural base for other molecules to bind to and associate with in lignocellulosic materials, forming a complex network. The interaction of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose enhances the functionality and complexity of lignocellulosic materials. The conversion of lignocellulosic material into green biofuels is hampered by the complicated arrangement of lignocellulosic components due to covalent and non-covalent bonding, but this can be lessened by using the right pretreatment methods. Pretreatment is conducted to eliminate hemicelluloses and lignin from lignocellulosic biomass, enhancing its accessibility for efficient enzymatic saccharification. Although numerous pretreatment techniques exist, each possesses distinct advantages and limits. Not every feedstock type can be pretreated using a single procedure. Microbes particularly break down lignin and hemicelluloses during biological pretreatment. Strong microorganisms used in pretreatment using biological agents include _Phanerochaete chrysosporium_, which produces many enzymes, such as laccase, lignin peroxidase (LiP), and manganese peroxidase (MnP), that selectively break down lignin [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib3). Different lignocellulolytic enzymes are secreted by naturally occurring bacteria and fungi, which aid in the effective breakdown of biomass and the synthesis of 5- and 6-carbon chain sugars. Biofuels and other goods with value additions can be made from these sugars. This review aims to provide a detailed overview of biological pretreatment approaches for lignocellulosic biomass, with a focus on their application in sustainable biofuel production. It delves into the pivotal role of biological pretreatment in improving biomass conversion by breaking down the complex structure of lignocellulose. Special attention is given to the enzymatic actions of bacteria and fungi, particularly in degrading lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose. The review further explores the strategic selection and optimization of microbial consortia, examines recent technological innovations, and outlines the key factors influencing the efficiency and scalability of these processes. By summarizing current research developments and future directions, this article supports the advancement of environmentally friendly and economically viable biofuel technologies derived from renewable biomass sources. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0005) depicts a simplified concept of biological pretreatment. ![Image 10: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr1.jpg) 1. Lignocellulosic material --------------------------- Lignocellulose is a renewable organic material that is plentiful on Earth. Based on the "waste to use" idea, which proposes using waste to generate energy, lignocellulose is used for the manufacture of ethanol [[4]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib4). The holocellulose and lignin molecules are strongly and intrinsically linked to form a three-dimensional structure. Aside from these three main components, lignocellulosic biomass contains trace amounts of minerals, phenolic compounds, acetyl groups, proteins, lipids, pectin, extractives, and ash [[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib5). The chemical makeup of LCB might vary according to LCB type (softwood or hardwood), lignocellulosic biomass variety, geographical area, and so on. LCB is a desirable substrate for the production of bioethanol due to its high cellulose concentration. The sort and quantity of non-cellulosic carbohydrates, as well as the presence of proteins or phenolic compounds, are all factors that affect the chemical makeup of LCB. Lignin is securely linked to cellulose or hemicelluloses, leaving the polysaccharides unavailable to cellulases or hemicellulases for digestion. The chemical makeup of lignocellulose is intricate and necessitates many pretreatment methods to achieve a fermentable sugar yield. The decongestion of lignocellulose is the initial crucial step to alleviate the stress on the downstream processing of this feedstock [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib6). LCB is the biggest natural carbohydrate source on the planet. The intricacy of turning LCB into a liquid fuel like bioethanol is associated with its resistance since they are made up of cellulose fibers encased in an amorphous matrix of hemicelluloses and lignin. ### 2.1. Cellulose The richest renewable resource on the planet is cellulose [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib7). Anselme Payen was the first person to isolate cellulose from wood after treatment with HNO 3 in 1838 [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib8). Cellulose is a structural constituent of LCB, or the glucose-based polymer, which is attached to either reduced or non-reduced ends via a β-(1–4) glycosidic contact ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0010)a). The tough and refractory character of cellulose is attributed to its high degree of crystallinity in structure, increased polymerization generally up to 10,000 units, and the existence of a complex network of inter- and intramolecularly hydrogen-connected hydroxyl groups. According to Houfani et al. [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib9), cellulose microfibrils usually have a diameter of 2–20 nm and a length of 100–40,000 nm. The rotation of C 6 H 12 O 6 units further facilitates the even distribution of ―OH along both sides of the chain, resulting in structural symmetry. The ―OH at C2, C3, and C6 of glucose units engage in both intra- and inter-chain hydrogen bonding. Along with other hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonding makes it easier for bundles of parallel cellulose strands to form. These are called microfibrils. Microfibrils combine to form fibrils, which in turn form cellulose fibres. The cellulose structure's three-dimensional lattice may show a variety of supramolecular structures, primarily in the form of amorphous and crystalline areas. The quantity of polymer is bigger in the woody portions of the plant, grain straw, and leaves. The non-woody tissues of the plant are normally poor in cellulose material; however, they acquire more richness with plant development. For example, cellulose content is only 15 % of the dry weight of herb plants and immature plants compared to 50 % in woody plants. It is reported that the cellulose level contained in many plants is between 15 % and 45 %. Remarkably, both cellulose and starch are polymers that share the same basic unit, glucose. However, the difference between glycosidic connections of the two types of polymers (alpha-1 and 6 in starch and beta-1 and 4 in cellulose) produces particular properties that favor the microbial attack on starch. In addition, the two sugars have fundamentally different associated microbial populations due to the many structural variations [[10]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib10), [[11]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib11). ![Image 11: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr2.jpg) 1. [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image") Fig. 2. Chemical structural units of (a) cellulose, (b) hemicellulose, (c) lignin. ### 2.2. Hemicellulose Hemicellulose is an amorphous, branching polymer comprising a minimum of 500–3000 sugar monomers. It is located in the main cell wall. The hemicellulose component provides rigidity to the biomass structure by serving as an adhesive between the cellulosic and lignin components. Cellulose and hemicellulose, containing reducing sugars, serve as significant sources of various economically valuable chemicals [[12]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib12). Hemicellulose is a branched heteropolymer. It consists of sugars containing five and six carbon atoms linked by glycosidic linkages. Hemicellulose also comprises numerous simple sugars, including pentoses and hexoses, with xylose being the dominant sugar ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0010)b) [[9]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib9). Furfural and furfuryl alcohol, which are utilized in chemical transformations like ethyl levulinate and γ-valerolactone, can be produced from hemicellulose xylose monosaccharides. Additionally, hemicellulose possesses exceptional properties that make it useful in a variety of industries, including polymeric materials, food, medicine, energy, and the chemical sector. Biodegradability, biocompatibility, and bioactivity are some of these attributes [[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib13). ### 2.3. Lignin The heteropolymer lignin provides crops with their stiffness and creates a sophisticated network inside cellulose microfibrils. In contrast, the lignin fraction is made up of extremely complex methoxylated phenylpropanoid units. It is primarily to blame for LCB's resistance. Lignin is a three-dimensional macromolecule composed of sinapyl, p-coumaryl, and coniferyl alcohols [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib14) ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0010)c). C–O–C and C–C connections bind these monolignols, which are also referred to as the S, G, and H units. A variety of aromatics with added value can be produced by catalyzing the lignin component. Peroxidase catalyzes lignin synthesis, which is mediated by radical coupling [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib15). It begins after the synthesis of holocellulose in the cell wall. Cross-links between lignin and hemicelluloses are formed during secondary reactions during lignin polymerization. One intriguing method for resolving the issue facing biorefineries is the biological conversion of lignin molecules produced during fractionation. Key steps in lignin bioconversion include pretreatment, ligninolytic enzymatic depolymerization, aromatic metabolism, and microbial synthesis of value-added compounds, just like in carbohydrate bioconversion [[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib16). Cellulose or hemicellulose can be hydrolyzed by holocellulases to produce hexose and pentose, which can subsequently ferment to produce C 2 H 5 OH. The polyphenolic combination known as lignin is incapable of fermenting. The chemical makeup of the several LCBs used to produce C 2 H 5 OH is displayed in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#tbl0005). Table 1. 32.57 | 10.16 | 3. Biological pretreatment -------------------------- For hydrolysis and subsequent separation processes to proceed as successfully as possible, biomass must be pretreated effectively. In order to accelerate protein hydrolysis (enzymes) and enhance the final amount of reducing sugars from cellulose or hemicellulose, the main goal of any novel pretreatment strategy is to change or modify the structural and chemical compositional [[22]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib22).]. In comparison to methods involving physical and physicochemical treatments, biological pretreatment remains relatively less studied. It operates at a slower pace, requiring an industrial-scale retention time of 14–16 days and potentially necessitating more significant spatial resources. Unlike controlled conditions, it lacks precise control. There is a chance that a lot of carbohydrates will be lost when lignin-degrading enzymes, cellulases, and hemicellulases are all made at the same time. Despite these challenges, biological pretreatment has garnered global attention from researchers due to its environmentally friendly nature and affordability. The complex and stiff component of LCB ([Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0015)), lignin, can be broken down by a variety of microorganisms that produce enzymes such as laccase, manganese peroxidase, and lignin peroxidase. LCB is broken down by a variety of bacterial strains, including Actinomycetes, _Candida, Bacillus_, and _Streptomyces_, as well as several well-known fungus species, such as _Ceriporia lancerata, Aspergillus_, and _Cyathus stercolerus_[[23]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib23). Because they can create enzymes that break down lignin, white rot fungi like _Pleurotus ostreatus, Phanerochaete chrysosporium_, and _Trametes versicolor_ are frequently utilized. According to previously published studies, a variety of microorganisms that increase the digestibility of organic matter were used to successfully delignify a variety of lignocellulosic feedstocks [[24]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib24). Biological pretreatments are conducted in a controlled environment and need less energy. Nevertheless, nearly all of these methods require lengthy incubation periods and are slow, which restricts their application in industry [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib25). Because they secrete lignin-degrading enzymes, including manganese-dependent peroxidase and lignin peroxidases, some white rot fungi are very effective at breaking down lignin during microbial pretreatment. As members of the basidiomycetes class, brown rot fungi mostly break down the cellulose in biomass, leaving behind dark wood as a by-product. While soft rot fungi associated with ascomycetes target cellulose and lignin, white rot fungi, which are also members of the basidiomycete’s family, target all lignocellulose components. The best fungi known for breaking down lignin in various feedstocks are those that cause white rot [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib26). ![Image 12: Fig. 3](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr3.jpg) 1. [Download: Download high-res image (107KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr3_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (107KB)") 2. [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr3.jpg "Download full-size image") Fig. 3. A conceptual model illustrating the bio conversion of LCB and its different applications. ### 3.1. Biological pretreatment by bacteria A variety of biomass-degrading enzymes produced by several bacteria are used in biological pretreatment. Because bacteria have a higher growth rate and metabolic activity than fungal species, their use in biological pretreatment may be more beneficial for reducing the length of pretreatment. Crucial steps in the manufacture of bioethanol include identifying the best bacterial strains for the pretreatment of lignocellulosic biomass, followed by enzymatic hydrolysis and fermentation. Cellulose and hemicellulose are a little easier to break down than lignin. Actinomycetes, α-proteobacteria, and γ-proteobacteria are the main types of bacteria that break down lignin [[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib27)._Cupriavidus basilensis_ B-8, a β-proteobacterium strain, can use lignin as its only carbon source and, after seven days, remove 41.5 % of the lignin [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib28).Additionally, certain aerobic bacteria, such as _Bacillus_ species, _Sphingobium_ species, and _Rhodococcus jostii_ species, are able to produce lignin-breaking enzymes [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib29).For example, a lot of study has been done on the cellulolytic bacteria _Cellulomonas fimi_ and _Thermomonospora fusca_ for the production of cellulase. Similarly, the cellulolytic bacteria _Paenibacillus campinasensis_ exhibits significant potential for treating lignocellulosic biomass and is resistant to harsh conditions [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib30).Every one of the at least 30 main rumen cellulolytic bacterial species—including _R. flavefaciens, F. succinogenes_, and _R. albus_ has a different way of attaching itself to cellulose and breaking it down [[31]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib31).Anaerobic bacteria like _Zymomonas mobilis_ are an important cellulolytic alternative that can be used in the fermentation of C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ and C₆H₁₂O₆ in order to create a high yield of ethanol [[32]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib32).Lignin is depolymerized by bacterial laccases produced by a number of bacterial strains, including _Bacillus subtilis_ and _Azospirillum lipoferum_[[33]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib33). Jiang et al. [[34]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#bib34) found that _Arthrobacter_ sp. C2 was a low-temperature resistant bacterium that could release Lip and MnP to break down lignin. The strain demonstrated activity in the pH range of 3–10 and reached its maximum degradation rate of 39.2 % at 150 C while using sodium lignosulfonate as a substrate. The carbon-oxygen bond may be broken, the methoxy group could be demethylated, the hydroxyl group could be oxidized, and the carboxyl group could be decarboxylated. The study results ([Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949823625000364#fig0020)) suggested significant degradation pathways that diverged from the breakdown process of mesophilic bacteria. ![Image 13: Fig. 4](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2949823625000364-gr4.jpg) 1. enriched_points.expanded_content: Sustainable practices in lignocellulosic biomass degradation are essential for conserving natural resources, minimizing waste, and reducing environmental impact, promoting long-term ecological balance.

enriched_points.point_title: Potential for reducing fossil fuel reliance
enriched_points.expanded_content: Utilizing lignocellulosic biomass can significantly diminish dependence on fossil fuels by providing renewable alternatives for energy and material production, fostering energy security.

Ongoing research into lignocellulosic biomass degradation is critical for developing innovative technologies, optimizing processes, and identifying efficient microbial strains for enhanced biomass conversion.

Stakeholders, including policymakers and industry leaders, must collaborate to invest in lignocellulosic technologies, support research funding, and implement sustainable practices for biomass utilization.
A sustainable future requires integrating lignocellulosic biomass into the economy, promoting renewable energy sources, and ensuring environmental health while meeting global energy demands.
